cards.grammarNote: الَّذِي (alladhī) = who/which/that (masculine singular). It follows a definite antecedent (الطَّبِيبُ) and agrees with it in gender and number. The verb in the relative clause agrees with the antecedent.

When the relative pronoun is the object of a preposition, a resumptive pronoun is required: بِهِ (bihi = by it/with it). الَّذِي أَقُومُ بِهِ = that I carry out (lit. 'that I stand with it'). This pronoun refers back to الْعَمَل.
Past tense is common in relative clauses. سَاعَدَنِي = helped me (سَاعَدَ + نِي suffix). The relative clause modifies الزَّمِيل (the colleague, masculine), so الَّذِي is used.

cards.front: the nurse who cares for patients (feminine)
cards.grammarNote: الَّتِي (allatī) = who/which/that (feminine singular). Agrees with الْمُمَرِّضَةُ (nurse, feminine). The four main relative pronouns: الَّذِي (m.s.), الَّتِي (f.s.), الَّذِينَ (m.pl.), اللَّوَاتِي / اللَّائِي (f.pl.).

cards.front: the engineers who designed the building
cards.grammarNote: الَّذِينَ (alladhīna) = who/which/that (masculine plural). Used for people. For inanimate plural nouns, use the singular feminine form الَّتِي: الْكُتُبُ الَّتِي قَرَأْتُهَا (the books that I read).

When the relative pronoun is the direct object of the relative clause verb, a resumptive pronoun is added to the verb: أُحِبُّهَا (I love it, f.). The هَا refers back to الْوَظِيفَةُ (the job, feminine).

cards.front: the student who passed the exam

cards.grammarNote: فِيهَا (fīhā) = in it (feminine). The preposition فِي (in) + هَا (it/her) forms the resumptive pronoun phrase, required when the relative pronoun is the object of a preposition in the relative clause.

cards.front: the office that I work in
the women who applied for the job
cards.grammarNote: اللَّوَاتِي (allawātī) = who/which (feminine plural, for people). Also اللَّائِي (allāʾī). For female humans specifically. تَقَدَّمْنَ = they applied (3rd person feminine plural past).
